does: This paper develops a SpeechLLM that jointly performs multi-granular L2 speech assessment and generates interpretable natural-language rationales. The model predicts ordinal proficiency labels at three levels—sentence-level dimensions (accuracy, fluency, prosody), word/phoneme-level accuracy, and explanatory text—in a single forward pass. Training combines supervised fine-tuning with Bounded Direct Preference Optimization using rubric-guided objectives. The authors evaluate on SpeechOcean762, demonstrating that their approach matches or exceeds single-granularity baselines while maintaining competitiveness with prior work. A key contribution is systematic analysis of rationale quality along two dimensions: self-consistency between explanations and predictions, and alignment with ground-truth labels. The findings reveal that rationales achieve plausibility at sentence granularity but suffer from sparse and weakly-aligned references at word and phoneme levels.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in instruction-tuned large language models for speech understanding and the broader trend of combining LLMs with speech encoders for complex audio tasks. It extends existing L2 assessment literature, which traditionally focused on single-aspect scoring or binary acceptability judgments, toward fine-grained multi-level evaluation. The hybrid training approach combining SFT and preference optimization draws on techniques from recent instruction-tuning and RLHF literature, adapting them for speech domains. The interpretability focus responds to known limitations of black-box speech assessment systems and aligns with broader calls for explainability in automated educational evaluation. The paper's analysis framework for rationale faithfulness—distinguishing plausibility from factual alignment—builds on emerging work in LLM explanation evaluation and grounding quality in generation tasks.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more transparent automated language assessment systems that educators and learners can understand and trust. The framework supports future research on improving faithfulness of explanations at sub-sentence granularities, potentially through better token-level label propagation or modified training objectives. The multi-granular prediction capability enables richer feedback for learners, supporting diagnosis of specific phonetic or fluency errors alongside holistic proficiency judgments. Work could extend to other speech assessment scenarios (native accent analysis, specific error types) or transfer across languages. The systematic evaluation of rationale quality provides a blueprint for auditing explanation reliability in other speech-language models. Finally, the tension between plausibility and faithfulness suggests future work on constraint-based decoding or dedicated fact-checking modules to ground generation in fine-grained acoustic-phonetic evidence.
